title: Naked Single
description: When a cell has only ONE candidate left, that must be the answer!
concept: After eliminating all numbers that appear in the same row, column, and 3×3 box, if a cell has only ONE candidate remaining, that number must go there. This is the most fundamental Sudoku technique.

steps.text: Welcome to your first Sudoku lesson! 🎉 A 'candidate' is a number that could possibly go in a cell. Look at the pencil marks — those tiny numbers in the empty cells.
Each empty cell shows all the numbers that COULD go there, based on what's already in the same row, column, and 3×3 box. Our goal is to find cells where only ONE number is possible.
Look at this highlighted cell. It has 3 candidates: {2, 7, 9}. That means three numbers could potentially go here. Not a naked single — but watch what happens!
See all these blue cells? They ALL have only two candidates: {7, 9}. This means 7 and 9 are 'locked' into these positions.
Since those blue cells lock up 7 and 9, cell (5,0) can't be 7 or 9. That leaves only ONE candidate: 2! This is a Naked Single — when only one number remains.
The answer is 2! 🎯 A Naked Single is when eliminating all other possibilities leaves just one number. The solver finds these automatically, but understanding the concept is key!

title: Hidden Single
description: When a number can only go in ONE place within a row, column, or box.
concept: Even if a cell has multiple candidates, a specific number might be 'hidden' — it can only go in ONE cell in a row, column, or box. When you find this, that number MUST go in that cell!

steps.text: Great job on your White Belt! 🌟 Now let's learn 'Hidden Single'. This is when a number can only go in ONE cell within a group, even though that cell has other candidates too.
Look at these two highlighted cells in row 3. They both have candidates {1, 6}. This is a Naked Pair — both 1 and 6 must go in these two cells.
And here — cells in row 6 also share {1, 6}. So 1 and 6 are locked in these pairs.
But wait — notice that 1 can ONLY go in cells 21, 22, 48, or 49. Looking at each row, 1 is a 'hidden single' in the sense that it has very few possible positions left.
In row 3, 1 can only go in cells 21 or 22. And 6 can only go in cells 21 or 22. These form a Naked Pair — but the key insight is that 1 is 'hidden' as one of only two options.
Hidden Singles appear when a number can only fit in one cell of a group. Here, with practice, you'll learn to spot them quickly! 🎯

title: Naked Pair
description: When two cells in the same group have the same two candidates, you can eliminate those numbers from other cells.
concept: If two cells in a row, column, or box have exactly the same two candidates, those two numbers MUST occupy those two cells. We can safely remove them from all other cells in the same group!

steps.text: Orange Belt time! 🧡 The Naked Pair is one of the most useful techniques. It lets you eliminate candidates even when you can't place a number yet.
Look at these two cells in row 3. Check their pencil marks — both have exactly the same two candidates: {1, 6}!
Since both cells can only be 1 or 6, one must be 1 and the other must be 6. We don't know which is which yet, but we know 1 and 6 are LOCKED into these two cells.
Same pattern here in row 6 — another Naked Pair with {1, 6}!
Naked Pairs are powerful because they eliminate candidates. If {1,6} are locked in two cells of a group, no other cell in that group can be 1 or 6. This narrows down possibilities everywhere else!
🎉 You've found the Naked Pairs! By recognizing that {1,6} are locked in these pairs, we can eliminate 1 and 6 from other cells — which might reveal hidden singles elsewhere!
